title: Needle-felted kingfisher
subtitle: A perching kingfisher in turquoise, cobalt, and orange merino, approximately 7 cm body length, needle-felted with layered colour work
excerpt: A perching kingfisher in turquoise, cobalt, and orange merino, approximately 7 cm body length. The iridescent blue-green back and wings are replicated by stabbing tufts of multiple shades over a core body, with a bright orange-rufous underside and white cheek patches.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted bird figure technique synthesised from general bird needle-felting practice. Kingfisher plumage based on the European kingfisher (Alcedo atthis).

glossaryTerms.definition: Combed and drawn wool fibres in a continuous rope used as the raw material for needle felting.
The visual shimmer created in felted work by layering multiple shades of the same hue over a base colour, replicating the light-catching surface of bird plumage.
A felting needle with small notches that tangle and compact wool fibres by mechanical action when pushed in and pulled out.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.

content.text: The kingfisher's colour comes from the contrast between its bright orange underside and its layered blue-green back. To build the
iridescence effect
, use at least three shades of blue-green: mid turquoise as the base, then patches of darker cobalt and lighter cyan stabbed over it with a size 40
barbed needle
. The
roving
colour sequence: body core first (any scrap wool), then orange underside, then turquoise back, then cobalt and cyan highlights, then white cheek patches and eye mask last.

items.name: Merino roving, light cyan or teal
Merino roving, dark grey or black (eye mask, beak base)
Core wool or scrap roving (body core)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Form the core body

Roll a compact oval of scrap core wool into a body approximately 6 cm long and 3 cm at the widest point. The kingfisher body is stocky: wide at the chest and abruptly truncated at the tail end. The head is a separate oval approximately 3 cm in diameter. Compact both pieces individually on the felting mat with a size 38 needle before combining.
Step 2: Apply the orange underside

Cover the entire underside of the body, from throat to tail, with orange-rufous merino. Work the orange up the sides to the midline and into the throat area of the head. Use a size 38 needle for the body and a size 40 needle for the finer detail around the throat. Keep the boundary between orange and blue-green at approximately the midline of the body.
Step 3: Cover the back and wings

Cover the back and top of the head with mid-turquoise merino as the base coat. Then, using a size 40 needle and very small tufts, stab patches of cobalt blue across the wing area and dots of lighter cyan along the spine line. Work the highlight tufts in short, shallow stabs so they sit on top of the base coat rather than blending into it completely. Step back regularly to check that the layered effect is building.
Step 4: Add the head markings

Attach the head to the body and work turquoise across the crown. Add a dark stripe through the eye area using very fine tufts of dark grey merino and a size 40 needle. Add the white cheek patch on each side: a small oval of white merino below and behind the eye area. The beak is a slender cone of dark grey roving, approximately 1.5 cm long, compacted to a firm point and stabbed into the front of the face.
Step 5: Attach the eyes and tail

Sew black seed beads into position on the dark eye stripe. The tail is a short, compact rectangle of turquoise and cobalt merino approximately 1.5 cm long and 1 cm wide, needle-felted flat and attached at the rear of the body at a slight downward angle. The kingfisher's tail is stubby compared to most perching birds: keep it short.

items.problem: The blue and orange bleed into each other at the midline
items.cause: Tufts were too large and the boundary colours were stabbed too deeply
items.fix: Work the boundary with very small tufts of the correct colour, stabbing shallowly. Define the midline with a single row of one colour before adding any tufts of the other side.

items.problem: The iridescence effect is not visible and the back looks flat
items.cause: Only one shade of blue was used, or the highlight tufts were compacted too deeply into the base coat
items.fix: Add small amounts of a third shade (pale cyan or electric blue) over the existing surface using shallow stabs. The highlight tufts need to sit on the surface rather than mix completely with the base.

items.problem: The beak is too short or too thick
items.cause: The cone was not compacted finely enough before attaching
items.fix: Roll the beak roving into a tight cone on the foam mat and use a size 40 needle to compact it to a sharp point before attaching. The beak should be roughly as long as the head is wide.

content.text: Finishing

Trim stray fibres with small scissors. The kingfisher can be mounted on a short section of dowel or twig, with legs formed from thin wire wrapped tightly with brown merino. The figure is most effective at or above eye level where the orange underside reads against a pale background.
